data.description: A little while ago, I was trying to animate an element’s background color, so that it cycled through the rainbow. Seems easy, but it turns out, browsers have a surprisingly big limitation when it comes to color processing! In this tutorial, we’ll dig into the issue, and I’ll share a couple of strategies you can use to work around this limitation.

(function() {
  const DEFAULT_MODE = 'light';

  let colorMode;
  let cookies = document.cookie ? document.cookie.split('; ') : [];

  cookies.some((cookiePair) => {
    const [key, value] = cookiePair.split('=');

    if (key === 'saved-color-theme') {
      colorMode = value;
      return true;
    }
  });

  if (!colorMode) {
    colorMode =
      window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')?.matches
        ? 'dark'
        : 'light';
  }

  // Ignore any values other than 'light' and 'dark';
  if (!['light', 'dark'].includes(colorMode)) {
    colorMode = DEFAULT_MODE;
  }

  if (colorMode === DEFAULT_MODE) {
    return;
  }

  // At this point, we’ve identified their preferred color mode, and it doesn't match the color mode used during SSR. We need to overwrite all of the colors.
  const colorMap = {"--color-text":"hsl(210deg 10% 90%)","--color-background":"hsl(210deg 15% 6%)","--color-blurred-background":"hsl(210deg 15% 6% / 0.75)","--color-muted-background":"hsl(210deg 38%   const d = document.documentElement;

  d.setAttribute('data-color-mode', colorMode);
  Object.entries(colorMap).forEach(([key, value]) => {
    d.style.setProperty(key, value);
  });

id="introduction" class="wcmb8ol"><span class="wlgbkjj">Introduction</span></a><p class="prg881n">Let’s suppose we’re building this particle effect:</p>
<div data-include-caption="false" data-alignment="center" style="--max-width:350px;aspect-ratio:350 / 225" class="w1hban68"><video autoplay="" loop="" muted="" playsinline="" src="/images/shared/like-button.mp4" aria-label="A “like” button that shoots out ~30 particles when clicked. The particles all have a pastel color and shift between colors as they fade away" class="vsmrqjy"></video><button style="opacity:0" class="ps5yn9o"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-pause"><rect x="14" y="4" width="4" height="16" rx="1"></rect><rect x="6" y="4" width="4" height="16" rx="1"></rect></svg></button></div>
<p class="prg881n">This effect is something we build in my <a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://whimsy.joshwcomeau.com/">brand-new animations<!-- --> <span class="p7a6phg">course<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:Rbg5eiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:Rbg5eiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a>, and there are lots of little details, but in this blog post, I want to zoom in on one surprisingly-interesting facet: the color shifting.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Each particle starts on a random color and changes hue as it fades out. This <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">seems</em> pretty straightforward, but appearances can be deceiving. In fact, I discovered a new CSS limitation I wasn’t aware of, and came up with a couple different workarounds.</p>
<p class="prg881n">In this tutorial, I’ll share what I’ve learned, and you’ll see how to build some super-cool color-shifting effects in CSS!</p>
<aside class="i6el1g6 b1btlj5u"><div class="dwkrqh8"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="28.5" height="34.5" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 57 69" preserveAspectRatio="none" class="sl0r50n"><path fill="var(--color-page-background)" d="M54 0V0.716804C54 25.9434 35.0653 47.1517 10 50L0 57V0H54Z" style="transition:fill var(--color-swap-duration) var(--color-swap-timing-function)"></path><path fill="var(--color-primary)" d="M56.9961 4.15364C57.0809 2.49896 55.8083 1.08879 54.1536 1.00394C52.499 0.919082 51.0888 2.19168 51.0039 3.84636L56.9961 4.15364ZM9.09704 51.7557L8.49716 48.8163L9.09704 51.7557ZM6 69V59.2227H0V69H6ZM9.69692 54.6951L14.3373 53.7481L13.1375 47.8693L8.49716 48.8163L9.69692 54.6951ZM14.3373 53.7481C38.202 48.8777 55.7486 28.4783 56.9961 4.15364L51.0039 3.84636C49.8967 25.4384 34.3213 43.5461 13.1375 47.8693L14.3373 53.7481ZM6 59.2227C6 57.0268 7.54537 55.1342 9.69692 54.6951L8.49716 48.8163C3.55195 49.8255 0 54.1756 0 59.2227H6Z"></path></svg><div class="f1dj028m"></div></div><div class="m1m3o2ch"></div><div class="iohjz9i"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="32" height="32" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-info"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10"></circle><path d="M12 16v-4"></path><path d="M12 8h.01"></path></svg></div><strong id="aside-intended-audience" class="t1xqw7pl">Intended audience</strong><div class="c690lmu"><p class="prg881n">This tutorial was written for developers of all experience levels. If you understand the fundamentals of HTML/CSS/JS, you should be able to follow along!</p><p class="prg881n">I do assume some familiarity with the <code class="i165vvr1">hsl()</code> color format and CSS keyframe animations, but I also have blog posts about both of those topics, and I’ll link them further down as they become relevant.</p></div></aside>
<h2 id="hello-color" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="hello-color-1" href="#hello-color-1" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Hello color</h2>
<p class="prg881n">So, in my particle effect, I want every particle to have its own dynamic colors.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Let’s start with the simplest possible option, generating a random color using RGB values:</p>

<p class="prg881n">The RGB color format supports 16.7 million possible colors (256<sup>3</sup>), and this code will select one of them, totally at random. Here’s what that looks like:</p>

<p class="prg881n">This looks alright, but we probably don’t want to use <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">completely</em> random colors. It would feel a lot more cohesive if all of our colors had the same general feel (eg. all pastel colors, or all neon colors).</p>
<p class="prg881n">It’s not at all obvious how we would do this with <code class="i165vvr1">rgb()</code>, but if we switch to <code class="i165vvr1">hsl()</code>, things get a lot easier. We could do something like this:</p>

<p class="prg881n">In this updated version, we’re picking a random <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">hue</em>, but we’re keeping the saturation locked to 100% and the lightness locked to 80%. This produces pastel-like tones:</p>

<p class="prg881n">This feels a lot better to me. The only issue is that randomness can be clumpy; there’s no guarantee that the selected hues will be distributed across the color wheel. But this won’t really be an issue when we start shifting between colors, so let’s not worry about it.</p>

8h.01"></path></svg></div><strong id="aside-color-formats-in-css" class="t1xqw7pl">Color Formats in CSS</strong><div class="c690lmu"><p class="prg881n">If you’re not familiar with <code class="i165vvr1">hsl()</code>, you can learn more about it in my blog post, <a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://www.joshwcomeau.com/css/color-formats/">“Color Formats in<!-- --> <span class="p7a6phg">CSS”<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:Rbgm95eiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:Rbgm95eiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a>.</p><p class="prg881n">That blog post explains how the <code class="i165vvr1">hsl()</code> color format works, but it also goes on to talk about more-modern color formats like <code class="i165vvr1">oklch()</code>. Modern color formats come with a couple of advantages:</p><ol class="oc96fnv">
<li class="wp18jjy"><div class="l1n7gg9f">They extend the range of available colors, letting us access more-vibrant colors on higher-end displays.</div></li>
<li class="wp18jjy"><div class="l1n7gg9f">They are designed to be perceptually-uniform, so a yellow with 80% lightness should feel as light as a blue with 80% lightness.</div></li>
</ol><p class="prg881n">For this particular problem, I don’t really benefit from either of those advantages. I don’t want my particles to be <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">super</em> vibrant, and I also don’t really mind if the blue ones are darker than the yellow ones. So, in circumstances like this, I prefer to stick with HSL, which has better browser support and feels more intuitive.<button class="t1cz8cv8"><span class="a1ni5s6f">*</span><span class="e2cu4f7"></span></button><span data-use-adaptive-colors="true" id=":Ri695eiavfelb:" data-direction="n" role="tooltip" style="flex-direction:column" class="p1lgzcrp l17lpp8k"><span class="p833mxt"><span class="pj0hw36"><span class="cffclov">Admittedly, part of this is just because I’m more familiar with <code class="i165vvr1">hsl</code>, but I think it actually is simpler. Modern formats like <code class="i165vvr1">oklch</code> are unbounded, meaning that there <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">is</em> no maximum saturation. This ensures they’ll remain compatible with future display technology with <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">even more vivid</em> colors, but makes them harder to reason about.</span></span></span><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="6" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 6" style="--origin-y:0%" class="s1vq0yeh s1k9z937"><path d="
        M 0 0
        C 6 0
          7.199999999999999 6
          12 6
        C 16.8 6
          18 0
          24 0
        Z
      "></path></svg></span></p><p class="prg881n">Here’s the same demo using <code class="i165vvr1">oklch()</code>, so you can see for yourself:</p><div data-use-adaptive-colors="true" data-demo-unit="true" data-control-position="none" class="o1m9cdug d16kq653"><div class="s1jc1lk2 w1v90hn6"><div data-can-be-annotated="false" class="wsu55tb"><div class="t1qrscf2"><div style="min-height:16.3125rem" class="w1gnkkgg"><p class="h13cj3qq">Sample Colors</p><p class="ekxm994">(<span class="mmypw1r">Click</span><span class="t1a4wfrh">Tap</span> the heart to generate some particles. <h2 id="shifting-between-colors" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="shifting-between-colors-2" href="#shifting-between-colors-2" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Shifting between colors</h2>
<p class="prg881n">Alright, so I’m happy with our single-color solution, but really our goal is for each particle to shift <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">between</em> two different colors.</p>
<p class="prg881n">As I mentioned in the intro, this was surprisingly tricky. Here‘s the first thing I tried:</p>

<p class="prg881n">In this updated version, I’m generating <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">two</em> HSL colors that are on opposite sides of the color wheel, and fading between them using a keyframe animation. I’m using <a class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="/animation/partial-keyframes/">a couple of keyframe tricks</a> that I wrote about a few weeks back.</p>
<p class="prg881n">I chose a linear timing function since it feels more natural in this particular case; each intermediate color is shown for the same amount of time.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Here‘s what this looks like:</p>

Even ridiculous values like <code class="i165vvr1">123456deg</code> or <code class="i165vvr1">-1337deg</code> will work just fine.</p></div></aside>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>What do you think about this?</strong> Take a moment to really examine it. Do the colors look good to you?</p>
<p class="prg881n">Personally, I found that the colors seemed a bit washed out. The particles become sorta grey-ish in between the source and destination. 🤔</p>
<p class="prg881n">This is clearer if we zoom in and slow down:</p>

<p class="prg881n">I would <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">expect</em> that the saturation and lightness would stay the same, and only the hue would shift through the animation, something like this:</p>

<p class="prg881n"><strong>Here’s the problem:</strong> when we animate <code class="i165vvr1">background-color</code>, the browser always does the calculations using the <i>RGB</i> color space, even when our colors are specified in HSL.</p>
<p class="prg881n">In RGB, we have three color channels (red/green/blue), each between 0 and 255. The browser does the math on these three channels <em>individually</em>. You can see why that’s a problem in this demo:</p>
<!-- --><div class="w1r66lnd"><div style="left:0" class="i1vg66il"><button style="background-color:#ff4d4d" class="c1skvvd3"></button><div style="left:calc(100% + 16px)" class="b1xflxhw"><div class="w1y7nvoh"><div class="r1xuh1hb"><div class="k67s48q">R:</div> <!-- -->255</div><div class="r1xuh1hb"><div class="k67s48q">G:</div> <!-- -->77</div><div class="r1xuh1hb"><div class="k67s48q">B:</div> <!-- -->77</div></div></div></div><div style="left:6px" class="i15704yz"></div><div class="busge9z"><div style="background-image:linear-gradient(to right, #ff4d4d, #f65656, #ec6060, #e36969, #da7272, #d07c7c, #c78585, #bd8f8f, #b49898, #aba1a1, #a1abab, #98b4b4, #8fbdbd, #85c7c7, #7cd0d0, #72dada, #69e3e3, #60ecec, #56f6f6, #4dffff)" class="b1ltaklf"></div></div></div>
<p style="color:var(--color-gray-700);font-style:italic;text-align:center;max-width:600px;margin-left:auto;margin-right:auto;margin-bottom:64px;margin-top:32px" class="prg881n"><strong>How to use:</strong> Click and drag to reposition the indicator, or focus the indicator and use the left/right arrow keys.</p>
<p class="prg881n">As we scroll from left to right, the red channel <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">decreases</em> as the green/blue channels <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">increase</em>. As a result, all three channels converge on the same value in the middle, producing a medium gray.</p>
<p class="prg881n">In other words, the browser is transitioning between these two colors using a mathematical model that doesn’t really have anything to do with how we perceive color 😅. No rational person would say that the midpoint between red and teal is grey!</p>
<aside class="shfe3ke b1btlj5u"><div class="dwkrqh8"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="28.5" height="34.5" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 57 69" preserveAspectRatio="none" class="sl0r50n"><path fill="var(--color-page-background)" d="M54 0V0.716804C54 25.9434 35.0653 47.1517 10 50L0 57V0H54Z" style="transition:fill var(--color-swap-duration) var(--color-swap-timing-function)"></path><path fill="var(--color-primary)" d="M56.9961 4.15364C57.0809 2.49896 55.8083 1.08879 54.1536 1.00394C52.499 0.919082 51.0888 2.19168 51.0039 3.84636L56.9961 4.15364ZM9.09704 51.7557L8.49716 48.8163L9.09704 51.7557ZM6 69V59.2227H0V69H6ZM9.69692 54.6951L14.3373 53.7481L13.1375 47.8693L8.49716 48.8163L9.69692 54.6951ZM14.3373 53.7481C38.202 48.8777 55.7486 28.4783 56.9961 4.15364L51.0039 3.84636C49.8967 25.4384 34.3213 43.5461 13.1375 47.8693L14.3373 53.7481ZM6 59.2227C6 57.0268 7.54537 55.1342 9.69692 54.6951L8.49716 48.8163C3.55195 49.8255 0 54.1756 0 59.2227H6Z"></path></svg><div class="f1dj028m"></div></div><div class="m1m3o2ch"></div><div class="iohjz9i"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="32" height="32" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-circle-check-big"><path d="M21.801 10A10 10 0 1 1 17 3.335"></path><path d="m9 11 3 3L22 4"></path></svg></div><strong id="aside-make-beautiful-gradients" class="t1xqw7pl">Make beautiful gradients</strong><div class="c690lmu"><p class="prg881n">I have another blog post that explains this issue in depth: <a class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="/css/make-beautiful-gradients/">“Make Beautiful Gradients”</a>. The first half of the post explains why we wind up with this “dead zone” in-between colors.</p><p class="prg881n">That post is about CSS gradients, not animated color changes, but really it’s all the same thing. We can think of our color animations as a gradient that takes place across <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">time</em> rather than space.</p></div></aside>
<p class="prg881n"><strong>There’s another issue, too.</strong>  Let’s say we wanted the color to rotate by a full 360°, looping around the color wheel. Check out what happens when we try to transition between 0° and 360°:</p>

<p class="prg881n"><strong>Nothing happens.</strong> You’re not doing it wrong. Hovering or focusing the button doesn’t seem to affect the background color at all. 🤔</p>
<p class="prg881n">In order to understand what’s going on here, we have to answer a <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">philosophical</em> question: are <code class="i165vvr1">hsl(0deg 100% 65%)</code> and <code class="i165vvr1">hsl(360deg 100% 65%)</code> the same color?</p>
<p class="prg881n">Well, they <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">represent</em> something different; <code class="i165vvr1">0deg</code> represents the very first color available in the spectrum, while <code class="i165vvr1">360deg</code> represents a full spin around the color wheel. But ultimately, both colors <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">resolve</em> to the same R/G/B values.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Unfortunately, as we saw above, the browser doesn’t do color interpolations in HSL; it first converts both values to <code class="i165vvr1">rgb()</code> and then transitions the values for all three color channels. And since <code class="i165vvr1">hsl(0deg 100% 65%)</code> and <code class="i165vvr1">hsl(360deg 100% 65%)</code> both resolve to the exact same <code class="i165vvr1">rgb()</code> value, we wind up not transitioning at all.</p>
<p class="prg881n">Fortunately, I found a solution that solves these problems, and offers better performance to boot. ⚡</p>
<h2 id="css-filters-to-the-rescue" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="css-filters-to-the-rescue-3" href="#css-filters-to-the-rescue-3" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>CSS filters to the rescue!</h2>
<p class="prg881n">If you’re not familiar, the CSS <code class="i165vvr1">filter</code> property provides SVG-style filters in CSS. For example, we can blur an element with <code class="i165vvr1">filter: blur()</code>:</p>

<p class="prg881n">One of the filters is <code class="i165vvr1">hue-rotate</code>. As you’d expect, this filter shifts the hue of a given element:</p>

<p class="prg881n">And here’s what that looks like:</p>
<!-- --><div data-use-adaptive-colors="true" data-demo-unit="true" data-control-position="none" class="o1m9cdug d16kq653"><div class="s1jc1lk2 w1v90hn6"><div data-can-be-annotated="false" class="wsu55tb"><div class="t1qrscf2"><div style="min-height:16.3125rem" class="w1gnkkgg"><p class="h13cj3qq">Sample Colors</p><p class="ekxm994">(<span class="mmypw1r">Click</span><span class="t1a4wfrh">Tap</span> the heart to generate some particles. The first few colors will be shown here.)</p><ul data-columns="2" class="l1gw5mdv"></ul></div></div><div class="b1cpro06"><button data-is-liked="false" data-variant="filter-flat" style="--pop-circle-duration:150ms" class="b12y7qoq"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" aria-hidden="true" width="48" height="48" data-is-heart="true"><path d="M3.68546 5.43796C8.61936 1.29159 11.8685 7.4309 12.0406 7.4309C12.2126 7.43091 15.4617 1.29159 20.3956 5.43796C26.8941 10.8991 13.5 21.8215 12.0406 21.8215C10.5811 21.8215 -2.81297 10.8991 3.68546 5.43796Z" stroke="white" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Like this post</span></button></div></div></div></div>
<p class="prg881n">It works! Each particle is able to rotate more than 180 degrees. 😄</p>
<p class="prg881n">Now, it’s not <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">quite</em> the same as changing the <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">hue</em> component of an <code class="i165vvr1">hsl()</code> color. Here’s a side-by-side comparison:</p>
<!-- --><div data-use-adaptive-colors="true" data-demo-unit="true" data-control-position="bottom" class="o1m9cdug d16kq653"><div class="w1v90hn6"><div class="r1qpi235"><div class="w1lolrdd"><div class="cy53ch8"><div class="l1g3thsf">background-color:</div><div style="--hue:0deg" class="h1wiatq b1g5ulsc">hsl(<!-- -->0<!-- -->deg 100% 80%)</div></div><div class="cy53ch8"><div class="l1g3thsf">filter:</div><div style="--hue:0deg" class="f1xp7lg b1g5ulsc">hue-rotate(<!-- -->0<!-- -->deg)</div></div></div></div><div style="grid-template-columns:min(400px, 100%);justify-content:center" class="d1j2qstn"><div class="s4h798d"><div style="opacity:1" class="h1pypve8"><label for=":Rk7eiavfelb:_hueRotate" class="lpsyt9t">Target hue<!-- -->:</label><span class="w1194iyg">0deg</span></div><div class="cxwqm2n"><input style="--handle-color:var(--color-gray-900);--handle-focus-color:radial-gradient(
    hsl(48deg 100% 75% / 1),
    hsl(48deg 100% 75% / 0) 40%
  ),
  conic-gradient(
    from 90deg at 50% 50%,
    hsl(45deg 100% 55%),
    hsl(52deg 100% 75%),
    hsl(45deg 100% 55%),
    hsl(52deg 100% 75%),
    hsl(45deg 100% 55%),
    hsl(52deg 100% 95%),
    hsl(45deg 100% 55%)
  );--background-color:var(--color-background);--base-handle-size:16px;--track-size:3px" type="range" id=":Rk7eiavfelb:_hueRotate" min="0" max="360" class="i1i8zjy7" value="0"></div></div></div></div></div>
<p class="prg881n">I’m not exactly sure how the <code class="i165vvr1">filter: hue-rotate()</code> algorithm works, but it tends to produce darker color variations. I’ve found it works best if I pick slightly lighter and less-saturated colors.</p>
<p class="prg881n">And honestly, I think two full rotations (<code class="i165vvr1">720deg</code>) is probably a bit much. I wanted to make sure the effect was clear in this demo, but in practice, I’d probably pick a smaller value, maybe something in the 180°-540° range.</p>
<aside class="shfe3ke b1btlj5u"><div class="dwkrqh8"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="28.5" height="34.5" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 57 69" preserveAspectRatio="none" class="sl0r50n"><path fill="var(--color-page-background)" d="M54 0V0.716804C54 25.9434 35.0653 47.1517 10 50L0 57V0H54Z" style="transition:fill var(--color-swap-duration) var(--color-swap-timing-function)"></path><path fill="var(--color-primary)" d="M56.9961 4.15364C57.0809 2.49896 55.8083 1.08879 54.1536 1.00394C52.499 0.919082 51.0888 2.19168 51.0039 3.84636L56.9961 4.15364ZM9.09704 51.7557L8.49716 48.8163L9.09704 51.7557ZM6 69V59.2227H0V69H6ZM9.69692 54.6951L14.3373 53.7481L13.1375 47.8693L8.49716 48.8163L9.69692 54.6951ZM14.3373 53.7481C38.202 48.8777 55.7486 28.4783 56.9961 4.15364L51.0039 3.84636C49.8967 25.4384 34.3213 43.5461 13.1375 47.8693L14.3373 53.7481ZM6 59.2227C6 57.0268 7.54537 55.1342 9.69692 54.6951L8.49716 48.8163C3.55195 49.8255 0 54.1756 0 59.2227H6Z"></path></svg><div class="f1dj028m"></div></div><div class="m1m3o2ch"></div><div class="iohjz9i"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="32" height="32" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-circle-check-big"><path d="M21.801 10A10 10 0 1 1 17 3.335"></path><path d="m9 11 3 3L22 4"></path></svg></div><strong id="aside-performance-win" class="t1xqw7pl">Performance win!</strong><div class="c690lmu"><p class="prg881n">Another benefit of using CSS filters is that they tend to be more performant than the alternatives. When we change <code class="i165vvr1">background-color</code>, the browser has to repaint each particle on every frame. With <code class="i165vvr1">filter</code>, the browser can reuse previous paints and instead apply a lightweight transformation on every frame, tinting the existing pixels rather than recalculating them from scratch.</p><p class="prg881n">If we only have 10-20 particles, the difference is negligible, even on low-end hardware. But at a certain scale, this would become a bottleneck, and I think it’s good to give ourselves that headroom! If we ever want to crank up the number of particles, we won’t run into any problems (at least, not with the color-changing part).</p></div></aside>
<aside class="i6el1g6 b1btlj5u"><div class="dwkrqh8"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="28.5" height="34.5" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 57 69" preserveAspectRatio="none" class="sl0r50n"><path fill="var(--color-page-background)" d="M54 0V0.716804C54 25.9434 35.0653 47.1517 10 50L0 57V0H54Z" style="transition:fill var(--color-swap-duration) var(--color-swap-timing-function)"></path><path fill="var(--color-primary)" d="M56.9961 4.15364C57.0809 2.49896 55.8083 1.08879 54.1536 1.00394C52.499 0.919082 51.0888 2.19168 51.0039 3.84636L56.9961 4.15364ZM9.09704 51.7557L8.49716 48.8163L9.09704 51.7557ZM6 69V59.2227H0V69H6ZM9.69692 54.6951L14.3373 53.7481L13.1375 47.8693L8.49716 48.8163L9.69692 54.6951ZM14.3373 53.7481C38.202 48.8777 55.7486 28.4783 56.9961 4.15364L51.0039 3.84636C49.8967 25.4384 34.3213 43.5461 13.1375 47.8693L14.3373 53.7481ZM6 59.2227C6 57.0268 7.54537 55.1342 9.69692 54.6951L8.49716 48.8163C3.55195 49.8255 0 54.1756 0 59.2227H6Z"></path></svg><div class="f1dj028m"></div></div><div class="m1m3o2ch"></div><div class="iohjz9i"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="32" height="32" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-info"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10"></circle><path d="M12 16v-4"></path><path d="M12 8h.01"></path></svg></div><strong id="aside-R4feiavfelb:" class="t1xqw7pl">Solving the problem without <code class="i165vvr1">filter</code>?</strong><div class="c690lmu"><p class="prg881n">When transitioning the <code class="i165vvr1">background-color</code> property, the maximum color shift we can do is 180 degrees. This is because the browser treats <code class="i165vvr1">hsl(0deg 100% 50%)</code> and <code class="i165vvr1">hsl(360deg 100% 50%)</code> as the same color.</p><p class="prg881n">I was curious if it was possible to solve this problem without using <code class="i165vvr1">filter</code>, and I was indeed able to come up with something, using the power of CSS custom properties. ✨</p><p Use left/right arrows.</span></button><div style="--desktop-flex:234.60000000000002" class="sm0fezx p1po48jq"><div class="r1w674po"><div class="w1n7upnl"><button class="a5rk396 tv02bcs">result</button><button class="tv02bcs">console</button><div class="a1puyu63"><abbr title="Refresh pane"><button style="transform:rotate(0deg);opacity:0.7" class="whty8xo a6j6o3o"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="16" height="16" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-refresh-cw"><path d="M3 12a9 9 0 0 1 9-9 9.75 9.75 0 0 1 6.74 2.74L21 8"></path><path d="M21 3v5h-5"></path><path d="M21 12a9 9 0 0 1-9 9 9.75 9.75 0 0 1-6.74-2.74L3 16"></path><path d="M8 16H3v5"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Refresh results pane</span></button></abbr></div></div><div class="r14pemjt"><div style="--iframe-background-color:transparent" class="r1lwkt8c"><div class="sp-c-euXojQ sp-preview sp-stack" style="min-height:360px"><div class="sp-c-juMdfR sp-preview-container"><iframe class="sp-c-fgviib sp-preview-iframe" title="Sandpack Preview"></iframe><div class="sp-c-kwibBT sp-preview-actions"></div></div></div><div style="--pointer-events:none;--opacity:0;--transition-delay:100ms;--child-delay:0ms" class="cvykehj"></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></div></div><p class="prg881n">If you’ve never seen <code class="i165vvr1">@property</code> before, it’s a technique we can use to assign a type to our custom properties (AKA CSS variables). If we let the browser know that <code class="i165vvr1">--hue</code> is an <code class="i165vvr1"><angle></code>, the browser can then animate this property when the value changes!</p><p class="prg881n">This is pretty complex stuff, so let me explain. On hover, we change the value of <code class="i165vvr1">--hue</code> from 0deg to 720deg. The browser will then recalculate the background color <em style="color:inherit" class="w10oesj0">on every frame</em> using this color, over the 2000ms specified with the <code class="i165vvr1">transition</code> property.</p><p class="prg881n">Before, the browser was only doing the HSL -> RGB math for the starting color and the ending color, which led to the limitation we saw above. With this new approach, the browser has to recalculate it on every frame, since it’s being dynamically built up using that <code class="i165vvr1">--hue</code> variable. As a result, we no longer have any issues!</p><p class="prg881n"><strong>Ultimately, this is a fun little bit of magic, but it’s not really the best solution.</strong> Our approach using <code class="i165vvr1">filter</code> is more performant and has <a rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank" class="a1tdgj4y s1j91s21" href="https://caniuse.com/mdn-css_at-rules_property">slightly better browser<!-- --> <span class="p7a6phg">support<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:0.7em;height:0.7em;opacity:1" class="s14qet46 s1gvsyfu"><mask id="external-icon-mask-:Rnc6cfeiavfelb:"><rect x="0" y="0" width="24" height="24" fill="white"></rect><rect x="10" y="0" width="16" height="14" fill="black"></rect></mask><rect x="3" y="6" width="15" height="15" rx="2" mask="url(#external-icon-mask-:Rnc6cfeiavfelb:)"></rect><path d="M 10 14 L 20 4 h -6 h 6 v 6" stroke="currentColor" class="amgfren"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">(opens in new tab)</span></span></a> (although both approaches are well over 90%).</p></div></aside>
<h2 id="twinkling" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="twinkling-4" href="#twinkling-4" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Twinkling</h2>
<p class="prg881n">Another small detail that makes a surprisingly big difference is the twinkling effect; as the particles fade away, they flicker a bit. It’s not a simple linear fade from full opacity to full transparency.</p>
<!-- --><div data-use-adaptive-colors="true" data-demo-unit="true" data-control-position="bottom" class="o1m9cdug d16kq653"><div class="w1v90hn6"><div class="w19amszz"><button class="bkjso1j"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" aria-hidden="true" class="swb35in"><path d="M3.68546 5.43796C8.61936 1.29159 11.8685 7.4309 12.0406 7.4309C12.2126 7.43091 15.4617 1.29159 20.3956 5.43796C26.8941 10.8991 13.5 21.8215 12.0406 21.8215C10.5811 21.8215 -2.81297 10.8991 3.68546 5.43796Z" data-liked="false" class="hyxdesd"></path></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Like this post</span></button></div><div style="grid-template-columns:min(400px, 100%);justify-content:center" class="d1j2qstn"><div class="s4h798d"><div style="opacity:1" class="h1pypve8"><label for=":Rkleiavfelb:_twinkleIntensity" class="lpsyt9t">Twinkle Intensity<!-- -->:</label><span class="w1194iyg">0%</span></div><div class="cxwqm2n"><input style="--handle-color:var(--color-gray-900);--handle-focus-color:radial-gradient(
    hsl(48deg 100% 75% / 1),
    hsl(48deg 100% 75% / 0) 40%
  ),
  conic-gradient(
    from 90deg at 50% 50%,

<p class="prg881n">One of my little animation secrets is to add small bits of random variation to everything. Each particle defines its own <code class="i165vvr1">--twinkle-duration</code> and <code class="i165vvr1">--twinkle-amount</code>, so that they don’t all flicker in lockstep like christmas tree lights.</p>
<h2 id="whimsical-animations" data-element-type="ContentHeading" class="hrk0sy8"><a id="whimsical-animations-5" href="#whimsical-animations-5" class="aax5dne"><span class="aieubfy"><svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="width:21px;height:21px;opacity:1" class="s1gvsyfu"><line x1="2" y1="8" x2="22" y2="8" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="2" y1="16" x2="22" y2="16" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="10" y1="2" x2="6" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line><line x1="18" y1="2" x2="14" y2="22" stroke="currentColor" class="l1etwsvd"></line></svg><span class="wlgbkjj">Link to this heading</span></span></a>Whimsical Animations</h2>
<p class="prg881n">If you’d like to learn how to build this full particle effect, along with <em>tons</em> of other cool animations and interactions, I have great news for you!</p>
<p class="prg881n">I’ve spent the past 18 months building a comprehensive, interactive online course all about whimsical animations. I share all of my most potent tricks and techniques. If you’ve ever wondered how I built some special effect on this blog, there’s a very good chance this course contains the answer. 😄</p>

metadata.author: Josh W. Comeau
metadata.description: A little while ago, I was trying to animate an element’s background color, so that it cycled through the rainbow. Seems easy, but it turns out, browsers have a surprisingly big limitation when it comes to color processing! In this tutorial, we’ll dig into the issue, and I’ll share a couple of strategies you can use to work around this limitation.
